Comunidad Indigena José Luis Caniulef

Case Study

33314415-10e1-8139-95b3-d391e432ca80, 33314415-10e1-81d3-b067-d98be432b808, 33314415-10e1-8198-9923-c18c439cb5e4, 33314415-10e1-814f-af27-fc7b5c118baa
background_section
## Background The Comunidad Indígena José Luis Caniulef descends from people who moved inland from Lican Ray on Lake Calafquén centuries ago. The name Caniulef—“fast vultures,” perhaps from spring flocks—stuck to this river-crossed forest. The place is Afunalhue: in Mapudungún, *afu* is what has ended or rotted and *alhue* is soul—a name for souls that once were here and no longer are, echoing traces of earlier inhabitants when the family arrived. The community sits in Wallmapu (Mapuche territory from ocean to ocean), at the edge of Chile’s Los Ríos region, near the Villarrica volcano, on [562 collective acres](https://youtu.be/R0G68ROe3U8) in the Pacific Ring of Fire. After the War of the Pacific, Chile and Argentina’s campaigns stripped and partitioned territory; roughly [10% stayed with Mapuche](https://youtu.be/R0G68ROe3U8) communities. Forests fell for export; later, dictatorship-era pine plantations and climate stress fed thirty years of megafires. At the head of the Cruces basin, Afunalhue faces tourism pressure, parcel sales, deforestation, polluted lakes, and mining—patterns repeated across southern lake country. ### Origins and Network José Luis Caniulef filed to protect Mapuche lands under Chilean law as the state pressed dispossession. José Luis Caniulef Huanquimil—leader and father of present members—defended the land on horseback and went to Santiago to secure title against Spanish-descended settlers. About [67 people](https://youtu.be/R0G68ROe3U8), [average age near 50](https://youtu.be/R0G68ROe3U8), contend with illness, aging and outmigration, weak territorial planning, frayed participation, poverty and few jobs, and fading biocultural memory. On collective land they farm, host visitors, and keep bees. Post-pandemic, the Afunalhue Project (2021–) asks how Indigenous knowledge and ecology can together track change and build resilience. It has [Ministry of Science funding](https://youtu.be/R0G68ROe3U8) across three linked lines of work.
Sources

patterns

Place
Parques de Afunalhue, 4930000 Villarrica, Araucania, Chile
place
Afunalhue, Wallmapu, Chile
activities
Oral History Collection and Memory Mapping|Ecological Assessment and Monitoring|Community Engagement and Education|Participatory Strategy Development
description
A Mapuche community in southern Chile working to document environmental changes, strengthen biocultural memory, and develop regenerative land management practices through the integration of traditional knowledge and scientific approaches.
vision_legacy_section
## Vision and Legacy The project has strengthened bonds under pressure. [One elder](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=EcpcXG1iGr0&t=7s&ab_channel=ComunidadIndigenaJos%C3%A9LuisCaniulef) likened rivers to community life: waters branch and reunite, sometimes stall, then move—history, memory, and ties remain the channel that orients them. What helps: spaces for youth, intergenerational work, and treating social knowledge, science, and education as one fabric—transdisciplinarity as a basis for regeneration. Leading from the territory and earning trust matters; so does moving from transactional ties or “being voters” to active stewardship. Hard parts include sharing technical results where many elders do not use phones or read. Dependence on external corporate-style funding risks cycles of obligation; the community wants stronger local finance rather than serving as a “last fix” for global harm. Participation can stay reactive; capacity stays thin without money; technical language is hard to land; and loss of Mapuche practice complicates intervention. The horizon is still clear: work across scales, follow water to the sea, and hold regeneration as shared work—neither simple nor one single agenda, but anchored in consensus that gives the effort meaning.
website
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=EcpcXG1iGr0&t=7s&ab_channel=ComunidadIndigenaJos%C3%A9LuisCaniulef
bioregional_context
Located in southern Chile at the boundary of the Los Ríos region, within the traditional Wallmapu territory. The area is characterized by high biodiversity, abundant water bodies, and evergreen forests. Situated near the Pacific Ring of Fire, with cultural and ecological significance tied to active volcanoes, particularly the Villarrica volcano.
category
Bioregional Organizer
